We had been noticing for a few weeks that there was some- but not a lot- of water left in our LG dishwasher (LDF7551ST) after every use. I cleaned out the drain lines and filters, but there was no improvement in the issue. After every time we would run the dishwasher, I would have to run the drain function manually to rid the dishwasher of water. I'm annoyed that this dishwasher has already had this kind of problem after less than 4 years of use, but this review isn't about the dishwasher- just the pump.

I had a feeling that this would be a very expensive repair if I called a service tech. A little research online and some youtube videos later, and I discovered that I could easily replace the pump on my own. Yes, it's not rocket science! I am a small woman, and it took me less than an hour to remove the dishwasher from the cabinet, remove the pump, install the new pump, hook everything back up, and put everything back in the cabinet. This pump works flawlessly for my dishwasher. And it's quiet, too! (*install tip- use vice grips to easily open those spring clamps!)

So if you're having drain issues with your LG dishwasher, skip the service call, save some bucks, and buy this pump. It's easy to install, and makes your dishwasher run like new.

The EZ-Scoop is used to load loose coffee grounds into the filter that is supplied for this purpose with my Keurig when I am not using pre-filled K-cups. The funnel part is folded back to scoop grounds into to the bottom portion and then swung on top to pour the grounds into the filter. It allows more controlled pouring of grounds into the filter than using a spoon or other type of scoop. Prevents spilling of grounds while filling the filter. I add a paper filter into the metal filter to make for quick cleanup of the filter after brewing. Paper filter and used grounds are easily thrown away and the metal filter is just rinsed with its holder between uses. The EZ-Scoop does not have to be cleaned until the end of the day. Advantage: If you like brewed coffee, this technique allows precise choice and good flavor from your Keurig. Not always the case with K-cups. Less expensive than K-cups. Disadvantage: Takes a bit more time than popping in a K-cup. But the flavor is worth it.

Bottom line: 30 days later, lots of loads later, and our dryer is humming away - with hot air! Perfect fit, and delivered a couple of days early. (The longer version of review follows) This was my first time taking on a major appliance. Diagnosis of problem was hardest part - everything seemed to be running/working fine, if wet clothes don’t bother you.
Figured out no heat was the issue. On that note, removing this component isn't very hard and it may confirm your problem early without any need for electrical current tester (I didn’t do this first). For instance, if you pull it out to inspect it, and pieces of metal coil are crumbling onto the floor or broken off, that’s a problem. But, learning to use a $15 circuit tester isn't too hard and may come in handy down the road. Almost ordered just a "fuse"; but, when testing the heating element for a closed circuit they say “disconnect all the wires from the Dryer Element box” - they mean all (top and bottom areas). I got a (false) completed circuit reading on the element the first time. Re-read directions, realized my error, and knew I’d found the problem. It was easily confirmed when I pulled the shield away from the dryer body - pieces of wire coils tumbled out. I ordered and received the part a few days early and in great condition.
Strongly recommend you search YouTube for video if you are a first time Do It Yourselfer. You'll probably see the person in video using gloves. Do it. There are MANY hidden rough edges and you will nick yourself. The dryer element was a direct fit. The hardest part was getting the "thermal cut off" removed from the old one... But, since I'd read up on possible points of failure, I had already opted to replace the Thermal Cut Offs (sold in a pair) and the Fuse since I was "in the neighborhood" (these parts tested okay. However, they are relatively inexpensive; and, apparently, common points of failure – so I considered this to be preventative maintenance. Even if you buy new Thermal Cut Offs, I suggest you remove the Thermal Cut Off from your old dryer element “box” - it will give you a good idea how to install your new one. This will make more sense what I mean when you see the way it "clips" in on one side. All in all - this part worked out great and brought me success - so, now I can add (VERY SLOW) Clothes Dryer Repairman to my resume.

I didn’t purchase through Amazon but I have this pitcher. It’s way too small. Experts will tell you that the volume of the milk “may” double when steaming to make a latte but I will tell you with a  DeLonghi Magnifica, it will MORE than double rendering this pitcher useless. Even if you are trying to steam “just” 6 ounces this pitcher will overflow and make a mess. The pitcher capacity is 12 ounces when filled to the brim which means there’s no extra space should your 6 ounces actually be 6.5. Further exacerbating the problem, this pitcher has no volume markings on the inside so now you have to clean the pitcher and a measuring cup. Not good. I have since bought a very hard to find 14 oz pitcher with markings on the inside which has been perfect for steaming 6 ounces. So why not an even larger pitcher? Like a 20 ounce? Because confining the milk a little builds better froth. Imagine trying to steam 6 ounces in a 2 gallon pot - it just doesn’t work. So, smaller is better but it needs to be “just right”. Expect the volume to more than double when steaming.

OK, so I have washed a load every day for three days. I must say, this little thing washes like a champ. I have put clothes in and the agitator really pushes them around. The hose that comes with it does not fit any faucet sold in America so it is best to develop a different means of filling the machine. So here are the pros and cons .

CONS: noisy during spin, needs to be watched while spinning, doesn't spin much of the water out, Although these things are a pain, they are a much smaller pain that dragging clothes to the laundromat, { I always worry about germs , bedbugs, etc., with a community laundromat.
So my feeling is that the minor imperfections do not outweigh the benefits. The price is reasonable, the thing washes well and the fact that the clothes take longer to dry is a small annoyance. If the spin was a little better I would give 5 stars.

I contacted GE Customer Service to ask them which broiler element my model JBP26WV2WW range requires. They looked it up and said I needed a model WB44X0185 replacement broiler element, which they would sell me for $75. I found this item on Amazon.com for under $17.

Some things you need to know.
- First, the item arrived in only 2 days -- not the 7-12 days that was estimated. The item was well packaged, arrived without damage, and also included 2 screws (in case yours are lost or too corroded to reuse)
- Second, I read a number of reviews where buyers expressed frustration with the screw holes in the mounting plate not aligning with the original holes in the oven. I did not have this problem. The holes aligned perfectly, so either the manufacturer corrected their mounting template, or their replacement part is identical to the original.
- Third, the electrical connections are not the slide-in type, but are the much more safe and secure screw-in type. Installation is trickier, but I was able to remove the old broiler element and install the replacement within 30 minutes. This included locating the right size tool to remove the 2 nuts holding up the ceiling mount, element mount, and electrical connections. (TIP: Before disconnecting the electrical contacts, wrap a long twisty-tie to each wire so that they do not slip back into the range. If this happens, you'll need to remove the rear panel of your range and push the wires back through the opening and into the oven so they can be attached. There are several DIY YouTube videos showing you step-by-step procedures for replacing your broiler element. Oh, and turn off the electrical breaker before doing any work on your range.)

While this replacement broiler element says that it will replace model WB44X173 and WB44X185 broiler elements, I can tell you that it's a perfect replacement for the model WB44X0185 part recommended to me by GE Customer Support (not sure why there's that added zero) and that the holes in the mounting plate align perfectly with those in my GE range.

So since this thing came into being, Keurig came out with a new type of coffee maker that won't work with this sort of K-Cup (it's the Supreme line, which uses multiple spray-nozzles instead of just one). If you have that sort of Keurig, you shouldn't get this and you're pretty much stuck getting the new model of the official My K-Cup. HOWEVER, if you have any other model of Keurig, you're in luck-- Especially if you have one of the Keurig 2.0, plus, or Vue models that had the DRM and hidden menus!

This K-Cup can be configured to use the standard "Classic" models, or the Plus/Vue models (like the 2.0 models). For the Classic models it's just a good custom K-Cup that will give you a nice brew. Quality build, easy instructions, probably the best non-Keurig brew-your-own cup out there. This works great in the K-Mini, K-Compact, K-Elite, K-Duo, and others of that type.

If you have a 2.0, Vue, or Plus model then you're in for a treat. This cup will bypass the DRM on the 2.0 models, and in models with hidden menus it can (at least in some models) unlock those menus and give you access to different brew sizes and options! And of course, it brews a good cup of coffee, as well.

It's quite a nice alternative to the official Keurig version. If you have a model that will work with it, it's definitely worth checking out if you want to save a few bucks. Five stars!

After years of frustration, both with function and taste, we decided to give up on our refrigerator's ice-maker and try something new. After reading the reviews, we settled on the EdgeStar Portable, and we have no regrets. We fill it with water from our filtered system and it makes all the ice we need, and that's a lot, since my husband puts everything over ice, all day, every day. Everything was working fine on this stove when it was new about 4 years ago but slowly over time I had to push harder and harder on the push button display for it to work.  After a year I was pushing the commonly used buttons several times just to get the thing to work.  After a about 2 years it became nearly impossible to use because even when I'd manage to get the bake button to activate, after punching in the baking temperature I was not able to get the start button to actually turn on the stove.  Some of the numbers would fail to work also at times so I was not able to bake at desired temperatures and had to use the next closest temperature I could get the buttons to work for.  At first I thought the contacts were worn out and thought I'd have to replace the digital display push button panel but after removing the ribbon cables from the circuit board to the digital control panel and re-connecting them again the control panel miraculously worked again.  I've now had to do this a second time.  It seems like this problem is progressive and worsens to the point of having an inoperable stove every couple of years.  I was originally going to give this stove 1 star for this reason but since the work-around only has to be done every year or two and is relatively easy I've decided to give this stove 3 stars instead.  I should not have to troubleshoot and fix my stove within two years of buying it and have to repeat this ever year or so.  The other problem is the heat from the burners is enough to slow warp the face of the plastic control panel over time.  Mine now has noticeable ripples. The plastic is permanently deformed due to heat.  The best thing about this stove is the center griddle.  It lets you cook two pancakes at a time and you can keep stuff warm on the edges.  It cleans up easily.  The oven bakes evenly - no complaints there other than it seems to cook at a bit lower temperature than what you think you are at.  I find I have to go 25 degrees higher than a recipe says or increase the bake time by about 25%.

My wife broke the plastic lock pin for the top-lid of our Kenmore Elite washer. I managed to superglue the broken off piece and it worked for a few months. Then she broke it again. Reminder to all: if the lid won't open (typically if you have switched off the machine before the cycle has completed) then DON'T pull on the lid. It will not open until the relay tells the little lock-pin to retract. Anyway, I wasn't able to superglue it again so I looked up the Model Number, input it in Amazon's Search box, and voila, there it was! Low price (same item can be $100+ elsewhere) and delivery the day after. Top class! Took about 30 minutes to replace. Once you know how to do it, it will only take 10 minutes. The hardest part is to find the two metal springs in the front between the body of the washer and the top. But stick/bang in two flathead screwdrivers on each side about 15 cm / 6 inches in from the edges. You will meet resistance when you hit the clip/clamp. Then just aggressively pry up the lid. It WILL pop up. Then just remove the back display part and remove the item you need to replace. Finally reverse engineer the assembly.

If your current dryer vent hose pops off the wall outlet every time you sneeze and you dread working in the tiny space between dryer and wall possible to reconnect it, then this is the answer you have been looking for. I spent literal hours cursing and adding foil tape to try to keep my "flexible" dryer vent hose secured to the wall outlet.

This product not only holds fast, even when I pull the dryer out a couple feet from the wall(!!) it also makes disconnecting the hose for cleaning and then reattaching super easy.

One note: I could not just place the seal ring inside the hose end and push it on. It would just fall right off again. BUT if you carefully work the seal ring onto your connect points (the wall outlet and the dryer outlet) then you can just push the plastic hose connector on and it stays!

I am about to have shelves put in that will make the side of the dryer inaccessible so being able to pull the dryer out and push it back in without worrying about that connection to the wall outlet is a must for me.

I am surprised by the negative reviews here. I have owned this dryer for over a year and my experience has been very positive. Coming from a conventional, vented gas dryer, I expected long, slow drying times but did not get this at all. We changed from large, conventional washer and dryer to the European-sized models so we don't cram every possible piece of dirty clothes into one load. I typically run 4-5 smaller loads per week: dark, light, white, towels and jeans, and dress shirts. This dryer does a great job of drying all these clothes types, especially if you take advantage of the special settings. For me, the key is to remove clothes immediately at the end of the cycle. This is true of all dryers, IMO, but especially with this one. It *does* leave a small amount of residual moisture in the clothing, depending on setting. If you pull clothes out while they're warm, then fold or hang immediately, almost all the wrinkles disappear without ironing.

If I have one complaint, it's that the dryer only rotates one direction. This means very large items (california king sheets, for instance) can get tangled and not dry evenly.

There is one MAJOR advantage to this dryer. Living in a drought-prone area, I put the drain hose into a bucket and recover one to two gallons of water from each load that I use to irrigate my garden. Clean clothes, happy tomatoes. What's not to love?

SETUP
Easy, just drape them over the top and sides of the washer and dryer. I like that they include two. Great. They fit ours. No real setup necessary

USE
Nothing to really use, they are made of nice material that is thick and protects the tops of the washer and dryer. It has magnets that keep it from slipping off. I felt that was a nice touch. What I didn't like was the pockets. For, is it just didn't work. Not sure why the pockets were there. They seemed impractical. Especially when we have our machines butt up against each other. We'll never use the pockets in between the machines, nor really the sides as well. We have shelves or that.

I say lose the pockets. Also, our dryer's lint trap is underneath the cover, so we have to move the cover to pull it out to clean it. Maybe we'll cut around it so that we won't have to remove it.

Anyway, these are decent covers.

I live in a 1300 sq foot apt and keep it set up in the bathroom near our entrance but even with the door closed and sitting at the other end of the apt I hear it banging so loudly I am concerned our neighbors will start complaining.  I have tried every load level since I initially thought I was overloading it or putting in unbalanced loads but nothing works...even with 2 pairs of socks it knocks around like a sledgehammer once it gets to the spin cycle.  Its so frustrating since I am technically not allowed to have a washer in our apt so having a loud banging noise makes the entire experience nerve racking. If I were to do things again I would have purchased another machine for more money with a less offensive spin cycle.
